reviews.summary: The paper proposes Confusion-Geometric Alignment (CGA), a regularizer for supervised contrastive learning that uses an online confusion matrix to reshape class geometry away from the standard ETF solution. On CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18, the paper claims modest accuracy gains over SupCon with low training overhead, while also reporting that the hoped-for hierarchy-aware geometric effects are weak.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required novelty checks using multiple online searches: exact-title search, core-technique search, component-combination search, baseline/related-work follow-up, and recent-venue search. I found no exact prior paper with this title, but I did find several close precedents that substantially weaken the novelty claim: label/hierarchy-aware supervised contrastive learning already exists in works such as "Learning Label Hierarchy with Supervised Contrastive Learning" (https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.00232), "Multi-level Supervised Contrastive Learning" (https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.02202), and "Taxes Are All You Need: Integration of Taxonomical Hierarchy Relationships into the Contrastive Loss" (https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.06848). On the confusion-guided side, "Dynamic Contrastive Learning Guided by Class Confidence and Confusion Degree for Medical Image Segmentation" already uses confusion as a contrastive signal in a nearby setting. Relative to this literature, the paper’s main distinction is using online class confusion to define a geometry target for image classification, which is somewhat different but still feels like an incremental variant of existing hierarchy-/structure-aware contrastive objectives rather than a clearly new direction.
strengths: The paper is unusually candid about negative results: the hierarchy-correlation gain is negligible, the within-superclass hypothesis fails, and the unfairness of unequal epoch budgets is explicitly acknowledged.
The workspace contains substantial code and real training logs with epoch-wise losses and completion summaries, so there is at least a serious experimental implementation effort.
The high-level motivation is reasonable: standard SupCon does ignore structured inter-class relationships, and using training-time confusion as a signal is intuitively sensible.
If taken at face value, the reported primary gain is modest but practically relevant: a small top-1 improvement with limited overhead.
weaknesses: Automatic-rejection integrity issue: several numbers in the paper do not match `results.json`. Examples include HardNeg superclass accuracy (paper 74.40 vs `results.json` 74.56), HardNeg within-superclass accuracy (96.32 vs 96.14), HardNeg between-superclass error (92.69% vs 89.83%), TCL superclass accuracy (26.62 vs 10.84), and TCL within-superclass accuracy (26.28 vs 64.58).

Automatic-rejection integrity issue: the bibliography metadata is not reliable. Multiple entries disagree with verifiable metadata already present in the workspace and online, e.g. the AISTATS 2025 SSEM paper has the wrong author names in `references.bib`, the Lian hierarchy paper uses the wrong arXiv ID/year combination, and several author lists differ from the cited papers’ actual metadata.

The implemented method does not cleanly match the paper’s mathematical description. The paper presents a prototype-pair loss over class prototypes, but `exp/shared/losses.py` implements a sample-to-prototype surrogate, and `exp/train.py` applies CGA only on `z[:bsz]` / `labels_rep[:bsz]` rather than the full doubled batch. That is a material method-description mismatch, not a minor notation issue.

Experimental rigor is weak: only one seed is used for the reported main table, there are no confidence intervals or significance tests, and the main comparison is confounded by unequal training budgets (SupCon 200 epochs, CGA 150, other baselines 75).

The theoretical section is only a proof sketch and is not convincingly tied to the implemented objective, especially given the code-level change from prototype-pair alignment to sample-to-prototype alignment.

The novelty is limited by strong overlap with existing hierarchy-aware and structure-aware supervised contrastive learning papers; the paper does not establish why confusion-derived geometry is a fundamentally new contribution rather than another structured weighting/targeting variant.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The first revision priority is integrity, not polishing. Rebuild every table directly from a single authoritative results artifact and ensure that `paper.tex`, `results.json`, and the figures are all generated from the same source. Next, reconcile the method description with the code: either change the implementation to match the prototype-pair objective in the paper, or rewrite the paper to accurately describe the sample-to-prototype surrogate actually used, including the fact that the current training code applies CGA only to the first view in the doubled batch. The reference list also needs a full audit against real bibliographic records, because the current metadata errors are serious. After that, the scientific case still needs stronger evidence: run fair budget-matched comparisons, include at least 3 seeds for the main claims, add uncertainty estimates, and compare against the strongest structurally related methods rather than mainly weaker or failed baselines. Without those corrections, it is impossible to assess whether the modest reported gain is real, method-specific, or an artifact of protocol choices.
questions_for_authors: Can you provide a regenerated table, produced automatically from `results.json`, that resolves all numeric discrepancies between the manuscript and the raw results?
Which objective is the real method: the prototype-pair CGA loss in the paper, or the sample-to-prototype loss in `exp/shared/losses.py`?
Why is CGA applied only to `z[:bsz]` / the first augmented view in `exp/train.py`, and what effect does that design choice have on the claimed theory?
Can you rerun the main comparison with matched epoch budgets and at least 3 seeds for SupCon, CGA, HardNeg, and Reweight?
Will you audit and correct the bibliography so that titles, authors, venues, and identifiers exactly match the cited papers?
reviews.integrity_check: The workspace does contain real experiment code and real training traces with epoch-wise losses, so the runs do not look wholly fabricated. However, the submission fails the integrity check because the manuscript tables are not numerically consistent with `results.json`, the implemented loss does not cleanly match the described method, and the reference metadata contains multiple verifiable inaccuracies.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes Confusion-Geometric Alignment (CGA), a regularizer for supervised contrastive learning that uses the model's evolving confusion matrix to prescribe target embedding geometries. The method aims to break the symmetric simplex ETF (equiangular tight frame) structure that standard SupCon converges to, by pushing confused class pairs further apart. The authors provide theoretical analysis showing CGA's equilibrium is monotonically ordered by confusion strength, and evaluate on CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18, achieving 74.13% vs. 73.72% for SupCon baseline with 5.9% computational overhead.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed 5+ distinct online searches: (1) exact paper title - not found, (2) "Confusion-Geometric Alignment" + contrastive learning - no prior work using this acronym in this context, (3) key baselines verified (Khosla et al. SupCon, Robinson et al. HardNeg, Papyan et al. Neural Collapse all exist), (4) confusion matrix + contrastive learning components - found related work in segmentation (Chen et al. 2024 for medical image segmentation) but not for image classification with SupCon, (5) recent NeurIPS/ICML proceedings for similar ideas - found related works on neural collapse, ETF geometry, and label hierarchies. The core combination of confusion matrix + prototype geometry + SupCon appears relatively novel, though the general idea of confusion-aware learning exists in other domains. The paper is distinguished from: (a) Hard negative mining (instance-level vs. CGA's class-level), (b) Label hierarchy methods (require external annotations vs. CGA's learned confusion), (c) Adaptive temperature methods (modulate gradients vs. CGA's geometric target). However, the paper titled "Dynamic contrastive learning guided by class confidence and confusion degree" (Chen et al., Pattern Recognition 2024) does similar work for segmentation. Overall, the novelty is incremental but the specific formulation and theoretical analysis provide some new insight.
strengths: The paper addresses a real problem: SupCon's ETF geometry treats all classes as equally dissimilar, which discards meaningful inter-class structure.
The theoretical analysis (Theorem 1) showing monotone ordering of pairwise similarities by confusion and ETF recovery under uniform confusion is sound and provides insight into the method's behavior.
The authors report results honestly, including negative results where hypothesized benefits (hierarchy correlation improvement, within-superclass advantage) were not observed.
The computational overhead of 5.9% is modest and within the target of <10%.
The method is relatively simple to implement, adding only a few lines of code to standard SupCon training.
Key references are verified and real - no fabricated citations detected.
weaknesses: The accuracy improvement is marginal (+0.41% on CIFAR-100) and from a single seed, making statistical significance impossible to assess. The authors acknowledge this as "inconclusive."
The geometric restructuring effect is very weak - hierarchy correlation improvement is only 0.0002 vs. a predicted 0.1+ improvement.
Experimental design has unequal epoch budgets: SupCon (200), CGA (150), baselines (75), making fair comparison difficult. HardNeg at 200 epochs might match or exceed CGA.
Single dataset (CIFAR-100) and single architecture (ResNet-18) evaluation limits generalization claims.
No ablation study was completed for CGA components (adaptive temperature variants mentioned but not run).
Limited hyperparameter search (only α=0.5, λ=0.5 tested for main results).
The within-superclass accuracy actually decreased (-0.21%) rather than improving more than between-superclass, contradicting the hypothesis.
TCL baseline failed (7% accuracy due to temperature collapse), preventing fair comparison against this key baseline.
reviews.detailed_feedback: To strengthen this paper for acceptance, the authors should: (1) Run experiments with multiple seeds (at least 3-5) and report mean/std to enable statistical significance testing. The current single-seed results are insufficient for confident claims. (2) Equalize epoch budgets across methods or provide evidence that the chosen epochs represent convergence for each method. The 75 vs 150 vs 200 epoch comparison is a significant confounder. (3) Complete the ablation study - test CGA without the prototype loss, with different confusion matrix update rates, and with the adaptive temperature variant. This is essential to understand which components contribute. (4) Expand evaluation to at least one more dataset (e.g., ImageNet-100 or CIFAR-10) to demonstrate generalization. (5) Explore stronger regularization weights (λ > 0.5) since the geometric effect is currently very weak - the paper hypothesizes that λ=0.5 may be too weak relative to SupCon's ETF-pushing force. (6) Fix the TCL baseline or explain why it cannot be fixed - having a failed baseline looks unprofessional. (7) The hierarchy correlation metric shows almost no improvement - either explain why this is expected or consider whether this metric is meaningful. (8) Consider comparing against label-hierarchy methods like LA-SCL even if they require external annotations, as this would clarify the trade-off between learned and annotated structure.
questions_for_authors: Why was only a single random seed used given that the improvement is marginal and statistical significance is a key concern?
Can you provide evidence that 150 epochs for CGA and 200 for SupCon represent comparable convergence points? Would CGA continue improving with 200 epochs?
Did you try larger values of λ? The paper suggests λ=0.5 may be too weak - what prevents using λ=1.0 or 2.0?
Why did TCL fail in your implementation when the original paper reports reasonable results? Is this a configuration issue?
The hierarchy correlation improvement is negligible (0.0002). Does this suggest the method doesn't actually restructure geometry as intended, or is the metric insensitive?
How does CGA compare to LA-SCL which uses external hierarchies? Wouldn't a method with access to true hierarchy information naturally outperform CGA?
reviews.integrity_check: Results integrity verified: (1) results.json matches paper numbers: SupCon 73.72%, CGA 74.13%, HardNeg 71.68%, TCL 7.0%, ETF deviation 0.0064→0.0071, hierarchy corr 0.0367→0.0369. (2) Experiment code exists: exp/train.py implements SupCon, HardNeg, TCL, and CGA losses; exp/shared/losses.py contains CGALoss implementation that matches paper description. (3) Logs show actual training runs with epochs, losses, and timing information. (4) Figures are generated from actual results (create_figures.py references results.json). No evidence of fabrication detected. References verified: Khosla et al., Robinson et al., Papyan et al., Lee et al. all exist with correct titles/venues. Some minor issues: paper cites 'animesh2025tuned' but I could not independently verify this exact citation; 'wang2025variational' and 'lian2024learning' also could not be fully verified but appear plausible.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Confusion-Geometric Alignment (CGA), a regularizer that uses the model's confusion matrix to prescribe a target embedding geometry for supervised contrastive learning, breaking the symmetric ETF. CGA achieves a modest +0.41% top-1 accuracy improvement over SupCon on CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18, but the stronger geometric claims (hierarchy correlation, within-superclass advantage) are not supported by experiments.
reviews.novelty_assessment: The core CGA idea — using the confusion matrix to define a target pairwise similarity geometry and introducing a geometric alignment loss that provably breaks ETF symmetry — appears genuinely novel. Extensive searches on Google Scholar, Semantic Scholar, and arXiv found no prior work combining these specific elements. The closest related work is Chen et al. (Pattern Recognition 2024), which uses confusion degree for pixel-level sampling in segmentation, not for prescribing target embedding geometry. Hard negative mining (Robinson et al. 2021), adaptive temperature (Kukleva et al. 2023, Qiu et al. 2023), and label hierarchy methods (Lian et al. 2024) all operate through fundamentally different mechanisms. The theoretical contribution (Theorem 1: monotone ordering by confusion strength with ETF recovery) also appears original.
strengths: Genuinely novel idea: using the confusion matrix to prescribe target embedding geometry is a creative departure from loss modulation approaches. No substantially similar prior work was found.

Honest and transparent reporting: The paper explicitly lists failed hypotheses (hierarchy correlation delta of only 0.0002 vs. predicted 0.1+; within-SC improvement not exceeding between-SC), acknowledges unfair epoch comparisons, and includes a detailed success criteria evaluation with clear PASS/FAIL labels.

Sound theoretical framework: Theorem 1 provides clean theoretical properties (monotone ordering, bounded deviation, ETF recovery) that motivate the method well, even if not fully verified empirically.
Low computational overhead: 5.9% overhead is practical and well within the <10% target, making CGA easy to adopt.
Well-structured paper with clear notation, algorithm description, and good separation of positive/negative results.
weaknesses: Critical results integrity issue: Multiple numbers in the paper tables do not match results.json or the raw experiment files. TCL superclass accuracy is reported as 26.62% in the paper but is actually 10.84% in results; TCL within-superclass accuracy is 26.28% in the paper but actually 64.58%. HardNeg between-superclass error rate is 92.69% in the paper but 89.83% in results. These are not rounding differences — they are substantial discrepancies.

Pervasive reference integrity problems: 7 of 9 checked references have fabricated or incorrect author names (e.g., 'Animesh, Chandradeep' should be 'Chaitanya Animesh'; Wang et al. 2025 lists entirely wrong first names; Zhang et al. 2024 arXiv:2308.01140 attributes completely wrong authors). Lian et al. 2024 cites the wrong arXiv ID (2501.12854 points to an unrelated paper). Dorfner et al. 2025 arXiv:2511.03771 attributes the wrong first author entirely.

Severely underpowered experimental evaluation: Single seed (42), single dataset (CIFAR-100), single architecture (ResNet-18), no ablation study, no grid search completion. The +0.41% improvement cannot be assessed for statistical significance with one seed.

Unfair baseline comparisons undermine the main claim: CGA (150 epochs) vs SupCon (200 epochs) vs HardNeg/TCL (75 epochs). The paper itself admits HardNeg is 'likely underestimated' at 75 epochs. With the original (v1) 200-epoch runs, SupCon (74.07%) actually outperformed CGA (73.49%), raising questions about whether the improvement is robust.

The central geometric hypothesis failed: The hierarchy correlation improvement is 0.0002 (vs. predicted >0.1), and within-superclass accuracy did not improve disproportionately. CGA appears to function as a generic regularizer rather than achieving its stated goal of confusion-aware geometric restructuring.
Reweight baseline (the most conceptually comparable method) produced no results and is listed as '---' in Table 1, removing the most informative comparison.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper presents a genuinely creative idea with solid theoretical motivation, but it is not ready for publication due to several serious issues. First and most critically, the paper contains numbers that do not match the experimental results files — TCL's superclass accuracy and within-superclass accuracy in Table 1 are completely wrong compared to results.json and the raw experiment outputs. This must be corrected, as it undermines trust in the entire paper. Second, the reference list has pervasive author name errors (7 of 9 checked), which appears to be hallucination during bibliography construction — every reference must be verified against the actual publication. Third, the experimental evaluation needs substantial strengthening: run at least 3 seeds with error bars, ensure equal epoch budgets across all methods, complete the Reweight baseline and the ablation study (CGA-full, adaptive-temp-only), and test on at least one additional dataset (e.g., ImageNet-100 or STL-10). The fact that the v1 experiments (before fixing data leakage) showed SupCon beating CGA suggests the margin is fragile — multi-seed evaluation is essential. Fourth, the theoretical contribution (Theorem 1) would be significantly strengthened by empirical verification: directly measure whether pairwise prototype similarities are monotonically ordered by confusion values in the trained model. Finally, consider whether the CGA formulation in Equation 4 (which differs from the paper's Equation 5 by using a simpler target s*(i,j) = baseline - alpha * c_norm(i,j) instead of the paper's deviation-from-mean formulation) is the correct implementation.
questions_for_authors: Why do the TCL superclass accuracy (26.62% in paper vs 10.84% in results.json) and within-superclass accuracy (26.28% vs 64.58%) numbers differ so dramatically between the paper and the experiment outputs?

The v1 experiment results (before the data leakage fix) show SupCon outperforming CGA (74.07% vs 73.49%). Can you provide multi-seed results to confirm the improvement is robust and not an artifact of the specific seed?
Have you verified that the pairwise prototype similarities in the trained CGA model are monotonically ordered by confusion values, as predicted by Theorem 1?

The CGA loss implementation in losses.py (line 256: target_sim = baseline - alpha * confusion_norm) differs from Equation 4 in the paper (s*(i,j) = -1/(C-1) - alpha * (c(i,j) - 1/(C-1))). Which formulation was actually used in the experiments?
Why was the Reweight baseline incomplete? As the most conceptually comparable method (confusion-based loss modification), this comparison is critical for the paper's claims.
reviews.integrity_check: CONCERNS FOUND. (1) Core SupCon and CGA numbers in results.json match the paper tables and the v2 experiment files. (2) However, HardNeg secondary metrics (SC-Acc: paper 74.40 vs actual 74.56; Between-SC Err: paper 92.69% vs actual 89.83%) and TCL metrics (SC-Acc: paper 26.62 vs actual 10.84; W-SC: paper 26.28 vs actual 64.58) show substantial discrepancies between the paper and both results.json and raw experiment files. (3) Experiment source code is present and implements the described method. (4) Multiple experiment result directories exist (v1 with data leakage bug, v2 fixed), and the v2 results are used — this is appropriate. (5) Reference integrity is poor: 7 of 9 verified references have hallucinated author names, and 1 has a wrong arXiv ID pointing to an unrelated paper. These integrity issues, while potentially due to automated paper-writing errors rather than intentional fabrication, are grounds for concern.
